item.title: Comment
item.content:encoded: Hiya, something bugs me. The SCP states that D-class personnel were "unable to leave hearing range, or to shut off the CD player or otherwise interrupt its function", without explaining how?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was my introduction to band The 5th Dimension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how some of the effects in the big list aren't explicitly stated how they're related to the number 5. It genuinely makes me want to do some research myself. I love the crazy ramblings of that guy at the end and how he kills himself is a huge shock. I kind of expect that he was under the effect of one of the discs but I don't know where the reference to the number 5 would be. All in all, this would make for the worst D&D Wild Magic table but it works as one of my favorite SCP's so far!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a Canadian historian I'm compelled to say that this article contains probably the only pop culture reference which has ever been made, or ever will be made, to Prime Minister Mackenzie Bowell. If I hadn't already upvoted when the guy talked about his suitcase full of garbage anomalies, that would've done it for me.

item.title: Tags
item.content:encoded:
(I would have noticed this much earlier if Wikidot still notified me whenever the page is changed)

in August of this year, the "fifthist" tag was added to this article.

I have removed it, as it is not applicable. There is no connection between the Fifth Church and SCP-092. Please do not restore it.

Thank you.

item.title: incidentally
item.content:encoded:
it's all just superficial Potemkin-village crap pumping imitation SHIT into the river of human achievement. It's Stein's fucking Oakland, and I don't even FUCKING understand those FUCKING allusions.

a Potemkin Village is basically a hollow shell.

and Gertrude Stein said, about the city of Oakland, "There's no there there".

thus, something as artistically null as the instances of SCP-092 would be "Stein's Oakland" - there's nothing really there.

item.title: Post Introspective Evaluation (post)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I liked this article. I really did. But now, after thinking about it for days I have realised the article, no matter how well it is written, no matter how creative or simple or (with an unraveling story telling patch at the end) as it may be, in my consideration it is not adequate to fit in the SCP Universe.

What happened to the times we as the Foundation used to write about interesting SCP objects that can not be stared at, or teleport away from you if in proximity? Some times we have, like in this case, the anomaly event, the story telling, everything, but then something lacks in cohesion. Perhaps the 5th dimension part, perhaps the 3125 albums, perhaps the anomalies caused by the SCP itself.

My suggestion to this article, if made any alterations, would be to rethink the concept and add some cohesion to it. Perhaps the anomaly still causes the mutations, or perhaps it still is something related to an old band from the 60s but both? Well it may work. Find some cohesion to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic, and I was reminded of Chris Morris' "Jam" by the ending. +5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was pretty good, but the ending was a bit silly.

item.title: Re: Mindbreak
item.content:encoded: My guess is the waves would cancel each other out and you'd be listening to silence. You would also explode, 5 times

item.title: Mindbreak
item.content:encoded: What would happen if you listened to every song on this at once

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty meta-honest

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would SCP-092-256 (the disc that makes you 2d) allow a person to interact better wish scp 085?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. He isn't a professional record producer, is he? Although if he were that would hardly be relevant; all of these are professional studio albums.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
also, count the exclamation points.

(I'm not sure it's fair to call -B a self-described hack, since he obviously didn't have full control over what he was saying.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• The standards of professionalism in pop album titles allow for considerable artistic license.
  • There is a band that is literally called "!!!" and they are as pro as you get in indie rock.
  • SCP-092-B isn't professional. He is a self-described hack.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I request the label to be changed from "The best of the 5th dimension!!!!" to something with less "!" Nothing professional uses more than one explanation point. I hope you take this into consideration.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I hear you. Keeping that in mind, I propose the following edit instead of the previous suggestion:

SCP-092-225 Listeners begin to vocalize song lyrics from the band "Constellation Starfish" while drawing five point star patterns on nearby surfaces.

Here we maintain a fifth church connection, one which is musically themed (fitting considering the nature of 092) as well as maintaining a pretty obvious 5 effect.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Maybe, but what would the "5" effect of the CD be? Reciting scripture just isn't obvious enough for Arrogant Cardboard.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: You want to go real obvious, maybe just a link to the Fifthist Hub?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: A Fifthist link, sure. But not 1425 — 1425's just a book, and the contents of its scriptures aren't obviously "five"-based enough for the hack who made these. What else you got?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'll think about how best to do so, but I wonder if you could throw in a link to qntm's Antimemetics series, seeing as that has a Fifthist link as well.

And in the interests of self-promotion, I'll note that Agent Dee is noted in A Convincing Lyre to have the highest number of 092 tests survived. Not sure how to work that in, but I'll consider further!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer removed. Edits not made per author request.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 72 hours if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, if there is one thing that this article references a ton, its the number five, so much so that I initially thought the fifthists were going to show up at some point before I got to the interview and learned it was actually AWCY? the whole time. That being said, something fifthist could be a neat easter egg in the 092 instance list, and would match the numeric theme. Suggesting the inclusion of the following entry on the 092 instance table:

SCP-092-225 Listeners begin to vocalize excerpts from the holy scripts of the Fifth Church.

Its a small reference, one keeping in time with the numeric theme of the article, and is, in my opinion, not very disruptive to the rest of the articles content.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i am really glad this was rewritten without being deleted. we keep all the history that way! wish more rewritten old SCPs went that path!

great job on this, +1 for sure! i liked the original but never rated it. now it's a definite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He took an anomaly that could teleport you to any celestial body, and fused it with a CD by a pop vocals group so that you have to listen to the CD in order to use it. Then he set it so it could only teleport you to Himalia, a frozen and near-airless lump of rock that's constantly being bombarded with lethal radiation from Jupiter. And it only works for 74 minutes at a time.

And this has no adverse consequences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but I just can't get behind this.

Aside from the object not doing anything for me (I get that that's the point, but the fact that the object itself is supposed to be boring doesn't excuse a boring story), the collapsible is way too long and waaayyy repetitive, with very lackluster effects; the only exception is the CD that transports you to a moon of Jupiter, but incidentally that's the most problematic aspect of the skip.

As interesting an effect as that is, I can't fit it into my head canon that some loser AWCY wannabe managed to do something that phenomenal, especially when you look at the rest of his handiwork. Also, it feels way too easy that you can instantaneously travel that far by simply listening to a CD, with no adverse consequences. Compare that to SCP-120, arguably one of the best articles on the site, and which, while it offers a way of teleporting people to the moon, does so at a staggering human cost. And that's just to go to the moon, not Jupiter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So reading this, a question pops into my head; if AWCY didn't toss this guy to the crows, what would he have done anyway? I imagine AWCY letting him down gently would arguably worse. I mean, this guy is an egotistical narcissistic who thinks he's better than the whole of a loosely-connected series of cells and chapters. I only shudder to think that had AWCY just said "Nah." or outright ignored him, he would have done something more drastic with his works.

Maybe he's a fan of John Cage as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, you just made this further blow my mind. This one just gets better and better as time goes on. Each reread of the scip itself and the comments is like a little gift. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it's not real, but I really want a copy of SCP-092-316. I am a big Doctor Who Fan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point taken (although I have a distinct memory of researching that beforehand and finding references to boron toxicity); I've switched it to manganese poisoning (for which I was able to find considerably more references).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps the best part of this is that, as far as I'm aware, 92 has nothing to do with 5 whatsoever. The Foundation really don't appreciate art.

+1 (If only because I can't +2)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of note, the very first test log is rather SoD-breaking. "Acute boron poisoning"? Elemental boron is non-toxic. It has an LD-50 of 6 g per kg of body weight. In other words, the average human would have to ingest somewhere on the order of a pound of boron to have a chance of dying. That's less toxic than salt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone should really make a tale about the exploration team sent to Himalia. That sounds really interesting. Maybe a few scips would even come back.

I like it a lot. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, now it works on even more levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just gotta say, I read the article, then started on the discussion thread… and it made no sense. So I looked at the history, and wow… it was just bad before. There was too much that just didn't… work. So I realized it was a rewrite. Then I went back to the comments and saw Voct was the one who did the rewrite. From previous entries I've read through, it seems Voct is the Savior of Bad Scips, and deservingly so in my opinion.

Well done, sir. Well done. +1

item.title: Re: Hidden details described in conversations
item.content:encoded: I read this comment, then looked again to see if you had quoted from 5 different conversations. Now I am disappoint. <sic> =(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't know you re-wrote this already, and now that I do, I can't believe it. This has just shot straight-up to being one of the greatest things I have ever seen on this site, I truly do wish I could upvote again

item.title: Hidden details described in conversations
item.content:encoded:
<Tuomey> "I justâ€¦ I'm not cool. I never will be. I'm really sorry about the mess. These aren't my arms." killed me
<Voct> killed him too.

<Voct> incidentally, the reason that he had to turn all his stuff over to the Foundation, instead of destroying it or letting it be destroyed
<Voct> that's because if it had been destroyed
<Voct> he could have taken solace in the thought that his stuff was TOO INTENSE for AWCY
<Voct> TOO EXTREME
<JackEnglish> ha
<Voct> they couldn't handle it, they had to destroy it
<Voct> by handing it over to the Foundation
<Voct> the least artistically-aware people ever
<Voct> his stuff will be locked up
<Voct> and taken out periodically
<Voct> examined
<Voct> dissected
<JackEnglish> I like that
<Voct> analyzed
<Voct> and never ever appreciated artistically
<Voct> the GOC would have destroyed the stuff as Too Dangerous
<Voct> MC&D would have auctioned them off as Peculiar Rarities to be Appreciated
<Voct> only the Foundation is emotionally dead enough for it to be a punishment
<Voct> I find that the best SCPs are those with more story than is included
<JackEnglish> is the forced reaction line "wow, that was cool" a direct shot at AWCY or am i misunderstanding it?
<Voct> that was absolutely a direct shot at AWCY
<JackEnglish> figured
<Voct> incidentally, it's "wow, that was *real* cool"
<Voct> (count the words)
<JackEnglish> hah
<JackEnglish> of course
<Voct> and it's that direct shot that got him killed horribly
<Voct> I had briefly considered having *five instances* of him going to five different Foundation sites at once
<Voct> but…. eh. Too much.

<Voct> he really really wanted to be an amazing artist
<Voct> he was rich
<Voct> he had access to all this anomalous shit
<Voct> he just wanted to be cool
<Uncandescent> Makes me think he could have done a lot of good in the world, if he'd tried to be constructive with his ludicrous amount of anomalies instead of trying to impress some super hipsters
<Voct> yep
<Uncandescent> I don't even hate him all that much for the implication that he was prepared to murder like, half the people who listened to his CDs

<Cardinal> The transcripted bits at the end are pretty neat
<Cardinal> Though his death seemed… anticlimactic
<Voct> a climax would have been impressive
<Voct> he wasn't allowed to do anything impressive

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's an interesting question — what do you think enraged them more?

  • that he made shitty art?
  • that he completely ruined anomalies that they could have used to make good art?
  • or that he was explicitly (and wrongly) declaring himself Better Than AWCY ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horribly overwrought for what are essentially CDs what kill you

Congratulations, you and the local chapter of AWCY? agree on something.

I think you might be missing some of the backstory here: the guy who brought the CDs to the Foundation was some bored hack with access to way too many anomalies who thought of himself as a True Artist, as good or better than anyone in AWCY?. He saw/heard of some of the things they made/did and thought "I could do that. ONLY BETTER." Unfortunately for him, he had all the artistic creativity of a non-anomalous rock, and suffered hard from the Dunning-Kruger effect, so he thought that he was making the bestest stuff EVAR, and presented it to AWCY? seeking their approval. He actually thought that all the stuff dealing with 5 and dimensions and so on was clever and original.

The local members of AWCY? were not amused. And apparently rather horrified at the monstrous waste of anomalies that he burned through, in his temerity at thinking he was as good or better than them. So they burned him, turned him, and tossed him to the wolves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horribly overwrought for what are essentially CDs what kill you, and the lack of hook isn't pardoned because AWCY. I don't find anything compelling in the interview log. This read to me more as a fifthist article what with the reality bending and the 5 motif.

Tell me if I missed something.

I think this needs to be shorter, especially the test log, the AWCY tie-in is uninspired and needs to be dropped, and this isn't helpful at all, but it needs a real hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahahaha wow. I'm rather impressed, I have to admit. You even managed to add AWCY to a classic SCP and make it work. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation even have multiple ways to generate new anomalies. They could make thousands every day if they chose to. They'd just rather not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
His cadaver isn't anomalous, in the way that a puppet isn't anomalous once the puppeteer pulls his hand out.

And the Foundation has scary-huge resources, yes, but they're relatively parochial in their scope. They don't go hunting for shit in other dimensions. This guy did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to upvote at several points, but I refrained from doing so until the end. Fanfuckingtastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Azzleflux: That's after AWCY got to him. Although I agree that seems a bit odd.

Drewbear: And he still got a huge number of them. Think how hard the Foundation works to scrape together their collection, and they've got country-scale resources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure that the fact that he managed to rip his own head off was somewhat of an indication that he may have been anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We also don't know that he had all the source anomalies before he started the project. It could've been "oh, lemme make these 5. …Okay, done. Y'know, 55 would be cooler. Crap, I gotta go find more things." And then he adapted each anomaly as he acquired it. At any given time, he may've only had possession of one or two anomalous things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this okay before, and was more than a little leery of incorporating AWCY?

But goddamn if that interview log isn't great. I wish I could upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3125 is 55 of course.

I actually mostly like this for the test log. The transcript is nice, but I wouldn't have upvoted just for it. The test log, it's methodical. Each effect is very simple and reliable, with the same trigger, so it doesn't stretch my suspension of disbelief to have so many of them. One Keter is probably harder to make than a hundred Safes. And the effects, they might be stupid, in-universe, but they aren't boring, and each is explored to its logical conclusion. I like it, I like it enough to upvote.

I suppose I'm not very cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you start your collection of anomalies with "keys to other worlds", you can pick up a lot of stuff surprisingly quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Drewbear: Sure, but I doubt he squeezed more than ten discs out of any given anomaly. That's still at least 300 anomalies, which is gargantuan. I could see him getting surprised by the AWCY folks attacking him, and not being clear on the fact that they actually are pissed until it's too late, but this guy is still basically the strongest entity in the entire SCP-verse.

Djoric: Voct said in chat (and it's indicated in the article), that he accumulated the anomalies he put into this through estate sales, etc. Anyhow, if he created the anomalies from scratch, that's an even more astoundingly off-the-scale amount of power here. This still doesn't address the incredibly short time-scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I can see multiple effects being derived from the same original source(s). Like anything that caused dimensionality changes like going 2-D or 5-D, or like the listeners turning into members of the band at different stages in their lives.

And the backstory is that the creator went TO AWCY? with these things, to prove that he was cool. And they were all like, "you monstrous hack. We're going to throw you to the wolves."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But the hipster was the one who made it. This was all just a big project of his, one big anomaly with 3125 parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like many things about the rewrite, but the scale is too vast. 3125 anomalies? Even accounting for the repetition, that's still several hundred, maybe a thousand anomalies that went into this, many of them pretty major.

Are we to believe that a private individual, however wealthy, accumulated a collection of anomalies on par with the Foundation's without anything bad happening, figured how to modify and use them in this thing, had the patience to undergo such a vast project, and did all this in only five years? And then, with probably the greatest private collection of anomalies ever assembled in the history of the planet, fell prey to some hipster goons?

The story is pretty good, and I like the dialog, but it's simply on too large a scale compared to the entire rest of the Foundation universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never heard or read it before. Of course, we have no idea where wassisname was picked up or what his/his handlers' native language is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this.

I love this forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit what the fuck just happened

Downvote changed to upvote, but I feel that the testing log dragged on for far too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't think there was a way to rewrite this one.

I was wrong five times over. Would upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Nekulturny" - I just know a lot of stuff about a lot of stuff. And AWCY has people from all around the world, and this guy is speaking the dialogue of more than one person at a time.
